title: Genuine Multipartite Nonlocality for Arbitrary Input: Maximal Randomness Generation and Robust Self-Testing
authors: Rajdeep Paul, Ranendu Adhikary, Alok Kumar Pan
abstract: Bell nonlocality provides the foundation for device-independent (DI) certification of quantum devices. We introduce a Bell inequality capable of identifying genuine multipartite nonlocality (GMNL) in an arbitrary m-partite scenario with an arbitrary odd number of measurements per party. Since the multi-setting nature of this inequality precludes the use of Jordan's Lemma, we construct an analytical sum-of-squares (SOS) decomposition to obtain the optimal quantum violation without assuming any bound on the Hilbert space dimension. This, in turn, enables self-testing of the shared entangled state and the corresponding measurement observables, up to local isometries, whose existence we confirm using a swap-based certification scheme. In addition, we show that our framework enables the extraction of maximal global DI randomness (m bits) at the optimal quantum violation, thereby exceeding previous limitations in the GMNL regime. Finally, we demonstrate that the architecture of our inequality yields improved robustness to noise as the number of measurement settings grows, ensuring experimental feasibility.
summary: The paper introduces a generalized Bell inequality to test genuine multipartite nonlocality across arbitrary numbers of measurement settings. Using advanced algebraic techniques like SOS decomposition, the authors derive the optimal quantum bound, proving that this nonlocality guarantees the extraction of maximal global randomness. Furthermore, they provide detailed analyses quantifying how robust these certified correlations remain when experimental noise is introduced.
main_problem: To develop a Bell inequality capable of detecting genuine multipartite nonlocality (GMNL) in arbitrary $m$-partite systems with an odd number of measurement settings ($n$), while simultaneously certifying maximal global device-independent randomness.
main_result: The framework successfully derives the optimal quantum violation, establishes strict separations between local/bi-local and quantum bounds, and proves that $m$ bits of DI randomness can be extracted at this optimum.
method: The authors employ an analytical Sum-of-Squares (SOS) decomposition technique to derive upper bounds for Bell functionals without assuming finite Hilbert space dimensions. Self-testing is achieved by analyzing the conditions under which the quantum expectation value matches the theoretical maximum.
model_or_system: The study analyzes $m$-partite quantum systems subjected to measurements with $n$ possible settings per party, characterized by a multipartite Bell functional $\Gamma^m_n$. The analysis extends to quantifying robustness against noise using trace distance calculations.
key_observables: Bell functional ($\Gamma^m_n$), optimal quantum violation ($\langle\Gamma^m_nangle_{	ext{opt}}^Q$), min-entropy ($R_{i_1...i_m}$), and trace distance between ideal and noisy expectation values.
important_parameters: Number of parties ($m$), number of settings per party ($n$, must be odd), noise parameters ($\epsilon, \alpha,  eta$), and the relative violation $r$.
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on SOS decomposition to bypass dimension assumptions. Robustness analysis often uses approximations based on small error margins (e.g., trace distance bounds proportional to $ eta$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate trade-offs between relative observed violation ($r$) and fidelity/trace distance bounds, showing how noise parameters affect the achievable quantum correlations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives upper bounds for local ($\Gamma^m_n|_L$) and bi-local ($\Gamma^m_n|_{BL}$) models. It then uses SOS decomposition to find the optimal quantum value $\langle\Gamma^m_nangle_{	ext{opt}}^Q$, followed by detailed sections on self-testing, randomness extraction, and finally, quantifying robustness against noise using trace distance bounds.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous mathematical tools (SOS decomposition) to certify fundamental quantum properties like nonlocality and randomness in complex multipartite settings, which is crucial for device-independent quantum cryptography protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
